body_txt: Being in a relationship can change people, and not always for the better — being in a relationship with the wrong person can often make good people go bad or bad people go even worse. Sometimes, this is a result of one person deliberately corrupting the another, while in other cases, it's a passive effect of being influenced by their lover's toxic behaviors. In some cases, both lovers might bring out the worst in each other. This trope could manifest itself in a number of ways: a good girl who gets with a bad boy might end up becoming a bad girl. A non-bigoted person might end up becoming a bigot because their lover is one. A wise person who ends up with a fool might end up emulating their lover's foolish decisions. Whether or not someone affected by this trope reforms usually depends on whether or not they break up with the lover; if they do, they usually will change for the better, either because they finally realize what a bad influence their lover is and break up with them for their own good or because their friends and/or family convinced them just what a toxic person their lover was. If a person refuses to break up with their lover, it will usually result in them falling down a dark path and suffering some kind of terrible fate either alongside their lover or said lover abandoning them either out of self-preservation or out of convenience. Sub-Trope of Love Makes You Evil . Often leads to a case of Took a Level in Jerkass or a Face–Heel Turn . Compare Lady Macbeth , Toxic Friend Influence , and Toxic Family Influence . Overlaps with Love Makes You Dumb if a person's lover influences them into committing foolish actions. Overlaps with Turned Off by the Jerkass if a character decides to break it off with their lover after realizing what a negative effect they were having on them. This kind of influence might be a justification for people who are Love-Obstructing Parents . Contrast Love Redeems and Morality Chain . Sister Trope to Destructive Romance , the distinction being that this trope involves someone's morals being negatively influenced without necessarily being harmed by it. Other characters may ask What Does She See in Him? if they see through the partner's "caring" façade. Since this trope involves people's personal relationships, No Real Life Examples, Please!
